author: @AllenEngel
text: Hello Mr. Murray-Smith :  Please speculate if the scintering device would scinter a TiO2 coating approximately 6 microns thick (0.25 mil). Thank you!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no it won't mate - it is very underpowered and meant just as a bit of hobby kit - it'll do non challenging materials like copper and GO

author: @rajankumar5651
text: What is the plug you used to connect the module to the outlet via computer cable.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Rajan Kumar mate - these are standard plugs - the kind that go into a wall socket household power outlet - it's whatever you country uses to do that - it's the same for any piece of equipment you plug in for power - like your TV - you don't use a computer cable -  this is very basic stuff -  i worry you shouldn't be attempting this project with the questions you are asking

replies.author: @rajankumar5651
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith  Hey Robert,


Thanks for responding. Actually I just wanted to know the name of the socket you used for the module because I couldn't make it out when you said it.


I am actually a graduate student at UCSD (San Diego) trying to develop wearable devices. At the moment, we have a challenge of sintering ceramic based particles and use hot pressing. The hot pressing method is expensive and time consuming, and the high temperatures of the process limit our substrate options. When I looked into photonic sintering, I noticed it can sinter particles at room temperature. I have tried to find simple solutions but they are too expensive and when I came across your video, I thought I could make it myself and considerably cheaper. 


I have been too precautious about taking this challenge because I am not familiar with the electronics but in order to accomplish what I need, I need to overcome the fear of the unknown. So While I appreciate your concerns, I will attempt this project but I will make sure I get some help from people on campus that are more familiar with the electronics. Or I would appreciate your help as well. 


I can provide more details through email or through a google hangout.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Rajan Kumar hiya mate - lol - sometimes the simplest of things can be the hardest to describe - good luck with the project

author: @robertposer574
text: Hi, very nice video! I think I tried the same xenon 750W kit in combination with copper ink from novacentrix so far without success - although having flashed the ink now more than 100 times the reduction does not seem to work. Could it be that the energy is not high enough?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: exactly right mate - you will need to increase power output - the draw on these things is huge if you want real power you will need more than one tube and connect them up to the supply on a separate circuit into the mains - better to use three phase - focus and area of exposure are also going to be important factors  as it is joules of energy per cm delivered that matters

replies.author: @ZhongweiLiu
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Thank Robert Poser bringing up copper ink. I am also trying to sinter silver ink with the same kit. no success so far. I agree with you that the power need to be increased by using more tubes and adding reflector to focus. I would appreciate if you can add another video to demonstrate that if possible. Thanks for nice video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Zhongwei Liu
sure - when i get round to it - but the basics are here - you could always do it and post a video

replies.author: @MrHariprashanth
replies.text: Robert Poser - Saw a publication where they used upto 45 J / cm 2 whereas I believe what we get here could be about a joule or so. If I have something successful will post a video.
